title: Lesson 45 — Appearances

words.definition: to pretend to feel or be affected by something
words.example: He feigned surprise at news he already knew.

words.definition: a false appearance or action intended to deceive
words.example: Under the pretense of a routine visit, the inspector searched the whole floor.

words.definition: a thing that is not what it is claimed to be; a fraud
words.example: The charity turned out to be a complete sham.

words.definition: a false or superficial appearance or manner
words.example: Behind the cheerful facade lay real exhaustion.

words.definition: an attractive but superficial appearance concealing something else
words.example: A thin veneer of politeness barely hid their rivalry.

words.definition: to pretend to be something one is not; to disguise oneself
words.example: The scam masqueraded as a government refund program.

words.definition: without deceit or cunning; honest and innocent
words.example: A guileless child who never thought to lie.

words.definition: innocent and unsuspecting; frank and open, sometimes naively so
words.example: His ingenuous questions revealed how new he was to the job.

words.definition: without cunning or deceit; natural and simple
words.example: Her artless honesty caught the panel off guard.

words.definition: stated or appearing to be true, though not necessarily so
words.example: The ostensible reason for the trip was research.

words.definition: claimed or alleged, often with the implication of doubt
words.example: The purported cure had never been tested.

words.definition: said to have happened or to be true, without proof
words.example: The alleged theft was never actually confirmed.

story: The consultant's whole business was one long [[feign|feigned]] competence. He kept up the [[pretense|pretense]] of expertise for months, but the company was a [[sham|sham]] behind a glossy [[facade|facade]] — a thin [[veneer|veneer]] of success over nothing at all. He would [[masquerade|masquerade]] as a former banker to new clients, correctly guessing that most of them were too [[guileless|guileless]] and [[ingenuous|ingenuous]] to check. His questions to investors seemed [[artless|artless]] and harmless. The [[ostensible|ostensible]] purpose of his firm — the one on the brochure — bore no relation to its [[purported|purported]] returns, and the [[alleged|alleged]] fraud came to light only when a client finally asked to see the books.
